title: Foreign Service Values & the 13 Dimensions
summary: The Foreign Service judges every candidate against 13 dimensions and a set of core precepts, and SJ items reward the response that best demonstrates those traits while following policy and proper authority.
content:
The State Department does not hire for technical skill alone. It selects and promotes against the Foreign Service core precepts and a published set of 13 dimensions that describe the whole officer: how you think, communicate, lead, and carry yourself under pressure. As an IT/Diplomatic Technology Officer, your network expertise gets you in the door, but SJ items measure whether you reason like a Foreign Service professional.

The 13 dimensions are: Composure, Cultural Adaptability, Experience & Motivation, Information Integration & Analysis, Initiative & Leadership, Judgment, Objectivity & Integrity, Oral Communication, Planning & Organizing, Quantitative Analysis, Resourcefulness, Working With Others, and Written Communication. Each names an observable behavior, not a personality type. Composure is staying calm and effective under stress; Judgment is discerning the prudent course among competing considerations; Objectivity & Integrity is being fair, honest, and free of bias even when it costs you.

• SJ items reward dimensions in combination. A strong answer to a server-outage prompt shows Composure (no panic), Judgment (triage by mission impact), Initiative (acts within authority), and Oral/Written Communication (briefs leadership clearly). Weak answers usually fail one dimension loudly — e.g., good analysis but no integrity, or initiative that ignores authority.

• 'Most effective' responses share a profile: they protect safety and security first, follow policy and proper channels, escalate when the matter exceeds your authority, communicate proactively, prioritize by mission impact, and preserve relationships. They are measured, not dramatic. They solve the problem AND respect the system.

• 'Least effective' responses also share a profile: they are unilateral (acting outside authority), concealing (hiding errors or risks), disproportionate (overreacting or underreacting), convenience-driven (easiest path over right path), or rigid (following a rule off a cliff when judgment was called for). Note that doing nothing and 'going to the top immediately' are BOTH often wrong — the test rewards calibrated action through the right channel.

• A common trap: the most aggressive or most thorough option is rarely the 'most effective.' The Service prizes Judgment and proportionality. The best answer is usually the one a respected senior officer would call sound, defensible, and collegial — not the one that maximizes any single virtue.

• Read the stem for the actor's role and authority. The same action can be best for a section chief and least for a junior officer. Identify what is in YOUR lane, what needs a supervisor, and what is the host nation's or another agency's call.
keyPoints: Score every option against the 13 dimensions; the best answer demonstrates several at once (composure + judgment + communication + integrity).
Safety and security outrank all other dimensions; never trade them for speed or convenience.
Act within your authority; escalate what exceeds it through proper channels rather than going unilateral OR going silent.
Proportionality is a dimension of Judgment — overreacting and underreacting are both 'least effective.'
Integrity is non-negotiable: a technically clever answer that conceals, misleads, or shows bias is a wrong answer.
'Most effective' = a sound, defensible, collegial course a senior officer would endorse, not the most dramatic one.
'Least effective' tells: unilateral, concealing, disproportionate, convenience-driven, or rigidly rule-bound.
Cultural Adaptability and Working With Others mean the host-nation and team impact of your action always counts.
Match the response to the actor's role; what is best for a manager may be wrong for a junior officer.
Doing nothing is a choice the test scores — usually poorly when action within your lane was available.

flashcards.front: What are the 13 dimensions the Foreign Service assesses?
flashcards.back: Composure, Cultural Adaptability, Experience & Motivation, Information Integration & Analysis, Initiative & Leadership, Judgment, Objectivity & Integrity, Oral Communication, Planning & Organizing, Quantitative Analysis, Resourcefulness, Working With Others, and Written Communication. SJ items reward several of these working together.

flashcards.front: In SJ scoring, what profile does the 'most effective' response usually have?
flashcards.back: Protects safety/security first, follows policy and proper authority, escalates what exceeds your lane, communicates proactively, prioritizes by mission impact, preserves relationships, and is proportionate. It solves the problem while respecting the system.

flashcards.front: What profile marks the 'least effective' response?
flashcards.back: Unilateral (outside authority), concealing (hides errors/risk), disproportionate (over- or under-reacts), convenience-driven (easiest over right), or rigidly rule-bound when judgment was needed. Doing nothing also scores poorly when action was available in your lane.

flashcards.front: Which dimension is Composure, and how is it tested?
flashcards.back: Composure is staying calm, focused, and effective under stress or provocation. SJ stems use outages, hostile users, or crises; the best answer is measured and methodical, never panicked or retaliatory.

flashcards.front: Define Objectivity & Integrity as a dimension.
flashcards.back: Being fair, honest, and free of bias even at personal cost — reporting your own mistakes, not favoring a contact who gave you a gift, applying rules evenly. Any option that conceals, misleads, or shows favoritism fails this dimension and is wrong.

flashcards.front: Why is the most aggressive or most thorough option often NOT the best answer?
flashcards.back: The Service prizes Judgment and proportionality. Overreacting wastes resources and damages relationships; the best answer is the sound, defensible, collegial course — not the one that maximizes a single virtue.

flashcards.front: What is Cultural Adaptability and why does it matter for an IT officer abroad?
flashcards.back: Working effectively across cultures and with host-nation norms and Locally Employed Staff. Even technical decisions (data handling, vendor choice, after-hours access) carry host-nation sensitivities, so the best answer weighs that impact.

flashcards.front: Why does the actor's role in the stem change the correct answer?
flashcards.back: Authority defines your lane. An action that is best for a section chief (e.g., reallocating staff) may be 'least effective' for a junior officer who should instead recommend it up the chain. Always read who you are.

flashcards.front: How do Oral and Written Communication show up in SJ items?
flashcards.back: The best answers brief leadership clearly and concisely, document decisions, and inform affected parties proactively. Options that solve a problem silently and leave stakeholders uninformed lose points.

flashcards.front: What does Initiative & Leadership reward, and where is its boundary?
flashcards.back: It rewards acting decisively to improve the situation — but within your authority. Initiative that bypasses your supervisor or the host nation crosses into 'unilateral' and becomes a negative.

flashcards.front: A prompt offers 'escalate to the Ambassador immediately' as an option. When is that wrong?
flashcards.back: When the matter is within your lane or should first go to your immediate supervisor. Jumping the chain is as poorly calibrated as going silent; the best answer escalates to the RIGHT level.

flashcards.front: What single test can you apply to pick the 'most effective' option?
flashcards.back: Ask: would a respected senior Foreign Service officer call this sound, defensible, and collegial? It should protect safety/security, stay in-lane or escalate properly, communicate, and be proportionate.

title: Teamwork, Diversity, Equity & Inclusion
summary: An embassy IT officer must collaborate respectfully across cultures and with Locally Employed Staff, foster an inclusive climate, address disrespect and harassment through proper channels, and speak up while still respecting hierarchy.
content:
An embassy is one of the most diverse workplaces in U.S. government: American direct-hires, family-member employees, Locally Employed (LE) Staff who are often the institutional memory of the mission, and counterparts from other agencies. IT touches everyone, so the technology officer's interpersonal conduct sets a tone. SJ items in this area test Working With Others, Cultural Adaptability, and Objectivity & Integrity.

LE Staff deserve particular care. They are professionals, frequently with deep local and technical knowledge, but they may face barriers of language, hierarchy, or status. The best officers solicit their input, give them credit, share knowledge rather than hoarding it, and never treat them as second-class colleagues. Dismissing LE Staff expertise or excluding them from decisions they should inform is a classic weak response.

• Inclusive behavior is active, not passive. The best answers ensure quieter or junior voices are heard, check that meetings and tools are accessible, avoid in-jokes or idioms that exclude non-native speakers, and distribute opportunities fairly. Equity means accounting for different starting points, not treating identical treatment as automatically fair.

• Disrespect and harassment have a bright line: you do not ignore it, you do not handle serious misconduct informally and quietly to 'keep the peace,' and you do not retaliate. The best response addresses minor rudeness directly and professionally, and routes harassment, discrimination, or hostile-environment behavior to the proper channels (supervisor, HR, the management officer, EEO/the Office of Civil Rights). Concealment and vigilante justice are both wrong.

• Building consensus beats winning arguments. The Service values officers who surface disagreement respectfully, find common ground, and bring the team along. Steamrolling a decision — even a technically correct one — damages relationships and is usually scored lower than a slightly slower, collaborative path.

• Manage in all directions. Managing up means keeping your supervisor informed and offering solutions, not just problems. Managing down means coaching, delegating, and giving credit. Managing laterally means coordinating with other sections and agencies instead of working around them. The weak pattern is going around people; the strong pattern is going through them respectfully.

• Respect hierarchy AND speak up. These are not in tension. The best officers raise concerns and dissent through appropriate channels — directly, privately, with facts and a recommendation — and then support the final decision unless it is illegal or unethical. Public undermining of a superior is weak; silent compliance with a flawed plan you never flagged is also weak.
keyPoints: Treat LE Staff as full professional colleagues: seek their input, share knowledge, and give public credit.
Inclusion is active — draw out quiet voices, avoid exclusionary idioms, and ensure tools and meetings are accessible.
Equity accounts for different starting points; identical treatment is not automatically fair treatment.
Never ignore harassment or discrimination and never handle it quietly to 'keep the peace' — route it to supervisor/HR/EEO.
Address minor rudeness directly and professionally; reserve formal channels for serious misconduct; never retaliate.
Build consensus and bring the team along; steamrolling a correct decision usually scores lower than a collaborative path.
Manage up with solutions, down with coaching and credit, and laterally by coordinating rather than working around people.
Respect the chain of command and still speak up: dissent privately with facts and a recommendation, then support the decision.
Give feedback privately and specifically; receive feedback without defensiveness and act on it.
Public undermining of a superior and silent acquiescence to a flawed plan are both weak responses.

flashcards.front: An LE Staff member suggests a better fix in a meeting but is talked over by an American colleague. What's the best response?
flashcards.back: Bring the suggestion back into the discussion and credit the LE Staff member by name. Inclusion is active: surface the quieter/overlooked voice and acknowledge their expertise rather than letting status silence good input.

flashcards.front: You witness a colleague making repeated demeaning remarks about another employee's background. Best action?
flashcards.back: Do not ignore it and do not handle it quietly to keep the peace. Address it appropriately and route it through proper channels — supervisor, HR/management officer, or EEO/Office of Civil Rights. Concealment and personal retaliation are both wrong.

flashcards.front: What does 'equity' mean in an embassy SJ scenario, versus 'equal treatment'?
flashcards.back: Equity accounts for different starting points and barriers (language, access, status) so people have a fair shot. Treating everyone identically can still be unfair; the best answer removes the barrier, not just applies a uniform rule.

flashcards.front: You believe your supervisor's rollout plan is flawed. How do you both respect hierarchy and speak up?
flashcards.back: Raise the concern privately, with facts and a concrete recommendation, before the decision. Then support the final call unless it's illegal or unethical. Publicly undermining the boss — or staying silent and letting it fail — are both weak.

flashcards.front: A teammate gives you blunt criticism of your ticketing process. Best way to receive it?
flashcards.back: Listen without defensiveness, thank them, separate the message from the delivery, and act on what's valid. Receiving feedback gracefully is a tested trait; arguing or dismissing it is a weak response.

flashcards.front: You need buy-in for a new security tool but two sections resist. Steamroll or consensus?
flashcards.back: Build consensus: understand their concerns, find common ground, and coordinate the rollout. The Service values bringing the team along over winning the argument, even when you're technically right.

flashcards.front: Why is 'working around' an uncooperative colleague usually the wrong answer?
flashcards.back: It damages lateral relationships and trust and often creates security or coordination gaps. The strong pattern is going through people respectfully — coordinate, escalate if needed — not bypassing them.

flashcards.front: What does good 'managing up' look like for an IT officer?
flashcards.back: Keep your supervisor informed proactively and bring solutions or options, not just problems. Surprising the boss or dumping unfiltered issues are weak; anticipating needs and recommending a course is strong.

flashcards.front: A junior technician on your team made a visible error. How do you handle it inclusively?
flashcards.back: Coach privately and specifically, protect their standing in public, and frame it as learning. Give credit for their good work openly. Public humiliation undermines the climate and is a weak response.

flashcards.front: Why are LE Staff often described as the 'institutional memory' of a mission, and what's the implication?
flashcards.back: American officers rotate every 1–3 years; LE Staff provide continuity, local knowledge, and technical depth. The implication: consult them on decisions, document with them, and never exclude them from matters they should inform.

flashcards.front: What is the bright line between handling rudeness and handling harassment?
flashcards.back: Minor rudeness can be addressed directly and professionally peer-to-peer. Harassment, discrimination, or hostile-environment conduct must go to formal channels (HR/EEO) — never minimized, never settled quietly, never retaliated against.

title: Decision-Making Under Ambiguity & Adaptability
summary: A strong IT officer makes sound, proportionate decisions with incomplete information — knowing when to act, when to gather more facts, and when to escalate — and adapts the plan as conditions change without lurching into paralysis or recklessness.
content:
Embassy IT problems rarely arrive with full information. A system is 'slow,' a partner agency 'might' have a deadline, a vague report of a possible compromise comes in at 5 p.m. SJ items in this area test Judgment, Information Integration & Analysis, Resourcefulness, and Composure: can you decide well when you cannot know everything?

The central skill is calibrating your response to two variables: the stakes (how reversible and how impactful is the outcome) and the clock (how much time you actually have). High-stakes, low-time situations demand decisive action on the best available facts. Low-stakes, high-time situations reward gathering more information first. The error is mismatching them — dithering when you should act, or acting rashly when you had time to learn more.

• Decide what you must know versus what would be nice to know. Gather the few facts that would actually change your decision, then stop. Chasing perfect information when a good decision is already supportable is analysis paralysis — a weak response the test penalizes, especially under time pressure.

• Weigh risk versus reward and reversibility. Prefer actions that are reversible, that limit downside, and that preserve options. A safety- or security-relevant unknown should be treated conservatively (contain first, confirm later); a low-impact unknown can tolerate a quick, reversible trial.

• Know the three moves and when to use each. ACT when it's in your lane, time-critical, and you have enough to be reasonable. GATHER MORE INFO when more facts would change the decision and you have time. ESCALATE when it exceeds your authority, crosses into safety/security/policy, or affects others who must weigh in. The weak answers pick one move reflexively for every situation.

• Adaptability is a scored trait. When new facts arrive or conditions shift, the best officer updates the plan rather than defending the original out of pride. Rigidly executing a plan that reality has overtaken is a classic weak response; so is abandoning a sound plan at the first minor wrinkle.

• Make your reasoning transparent and document key assumptions. State the trade-offs, note what you assumed and what would trigger a change of course, and tell affected stakeholders. A defensible decision under ambiguity is one others can understand and revisit — not a guess made in silence.

• Balance thoroughness with timeliness. The best answer is rarely the most exhaustive option; it is the one that is good enough, on time, and adjustable. Recklessness (acting with no basis on an irreversible matter) and paralysis (endless study of a time-critical one) are the two failure modes to avoid.
keyPoints: Calibrate to stakes and clock: high-impact + low-time = act decisively; low-impact + high-time = learn more first.
Gather only the facts that would change your decision, then commit — chasing perfect information is analysis paralysis.
Prefer reversible, downside-limiting actions; treat safety/security unknowns conservatively (contain first, confirm later).
Use the right move: ACT in your lane and time-critical, GATHER when facts would change the call, ESCALATE when it exceeds your authority.
Adapt when conditions or facts change; defending an overtaken plan out of pride is a weak response.
Don't abandon a sound plan at the first minor wrinkle — adaptability is calibrated, not flighty.
Make assumptions and trade-offs explicit, and define what would trigger a change of course.
Inform affected stakeholders so the decision is transparent and can be revisited, not made in silence.
Avoid both failure modes: recklessness on irreversible matters and paralysis on time-critical ones.
'Good enough, on time, and adjustable' usually beats the most exhaustive option.

flashcards.front: A vague after-hours report says a workstation 'might' be compromised; you can't confirm tonight. Act or wait?
flashcards.back: Treat the security unknown conservatively: contain first (isolate the workstation) and confirm later. High-stakes, security-relevant ambiguity demands a reversible protective action now, not waiting for perfect confirmation.

flashcards.front: How do you decide whether to gather more information or commit to a decision?
flashcards.back: Ask whether the missing facts would actually change your decision and whether you have time. If yes and you have time, gather them; if not, commit. Chasing nice-to-know facts under time pressure is analysis paralysis.

flashcards.front: What two variables should you calibrate your response to under ambiguity?
flashcards.back: Stakes (impact and reversibility) and the clock (time available). High-stakes + low-time = decisive action on best facts; low-stakes + high-time = gather more first. The error is mismatching action to the situation.

flashcards.front: When is ESCALATE the right move under uncertainty?
flashcards.back: When the matter exceeds your authority, touches safety/security/policy, or affects others who must weigh in. Escalating in-lane trivia is weak; failing to escalate something above your authority is also weak.

flashcards.front: New information arrives that undercuts the plan you committed to an hour ago. Best response?
flashcards.back: Update the plan. Adaptability is a scored trait; defending an overtaken plan out of pride or sunk cost is a classic weak response. Acknowledge the change and adjust course transparently.

flashcards.front: Why is 'do the most thorough, exhaustive analysis' often NOT the best option in SJ items?
flashcards.back: Because timeliness is also a value. The best answer is usually good enough, on time, and adjustable. Exhaustive analysis that misses the window is analysis paralysis and scores poorly.

flashcards.front: What makes a decision under ambiguity 'defensible'?
flashcards.back: It rests on the facts that mattered, states assumptions and trade-offs, prefers reversible options, informs stakeholders, and defines what would trigger a change of course — so others can understand and revisit it.

flashcards.front: You face a low-impact, easily reversible choice and have plenty of time. What's the trap?
flashcards.back: Over-escalating or over-analyzing it. Match effort to stakes: a quick, reversible trial or a simple in-lane decision is appropriate; turning a minor matter into a major process is a weak, disproportionate response.

flashcards.front: Define the two failure modes this dimension warns against.
flashcards.back: Recklessness — acting with no real basis on an irreversible, high-stakes matter — and paralysis — endlessly studying a time-critical one. Good judgment under ambiguity steers between them.

flashcards.front: How does reversibility shape risk-vs-reward decisions?
flashcards.back: Reversible actions are cheaper to be wrong about, so you can move faster and learn by doing. Irreversible, high-impact actions warrant more information and often escalation before committing.

flashcards.front: Why document your assumptions when deciding with incomplete information?
flashcards.back: So the decision is transparent and revisable: others know what you knew, can challenge the assumptions, and can recognize when a triggering condition for changing course has occurred. Silent guessing isn't defensible.

flashcards.front: A partner agency 'might' have a deadline tomorrow but you can't reach them. How do you proceed?
flashcards.back: Make a reasonable, reversible plan that protects the likely deadline while you confirm — and keep trying to reach them and your supervisor. Don't gamble that there's no deadline, and don't freeze waiting for certainty.

title: Stewardship, Accountability & Continuity Planning
summary: A trustworthy IT officer reduces risk before it becomes a crisis, documents and transfers knowledge, plans for continuity of operations, protects government resources, reports waste/fraud/abuse appropriately, and turns every incident into a lesson learned.
content:
Stewardship is the quiet discipline behind crisis response: the work you do before, between, and after emergencies so the mission keeps running and public resources are protected. SJ items here test Objectivity & Integrity, Planning & Organizing, Initiative, and Judgment. The through-line is accountability — owning the systems, the funds, and the institutional knowledge entrusted to you.

Proactive risk reduction means you don't wait for the outage. You patch, back up, test restores, review access, and fix the single points of failure you can see. The strongest officers treat near-misses as free lessons and close gaps before they're exploited. The weak pattern is reactive-only: heroics during the fire, nothing to prevent the next one.

• Documentation and knowledge transfer are stewardship, not bureaucracy. In a rotational service where officers leave every few years, undocumented systems are a liability. The best officers write runbooks, label systems, cross-train colleagues and LE Staff, and ensure no critical capability lives only in one person's head. Hoarding knowledge for job security is a weak, self-serving response.

• Continuity of Operations (COOP) is a mindset: assume systems will fail and plan how the mission continues anyway. That means tested backups (a backup you've never restored is a hope, not a backup), offline fallbacks, communication trees, and clear recovery priorities tied to mission impact. The best answers ask 'how do we keep operating?' not just 'how do we fix this?'

• Protect government resources and funds as if personally accountable — because you are. Don't over-buy, don't let licenses or equipment go to waste, don't use government IT or funds for unofficial purposes, and steward property and data carefully. Convenience-driven misuse of resources is a weak response even when no one is watching.

• Report waste, fraud, and abuse through proper channels. If you see misused funds, falsified records, or serious misconduct, integrity requires reporting it — to your supervisor, management, the Inspector General (OIG), or other appropriate channels — not ignoring it, not confronting it vigilante-style, and not joining in. Whistleblower protections exist for this; concealment is the wrong answer.

• Learn from incidents with a blameless after-action review. Once the emergency is over, the steward asks what happened, why, and what systemic change prevents recurrence — documenting findings and following through. Blaming individuals to close the ticket, or skipping the review entirely because things are working again, are both weak responses. Integrity over expediency: do the durable fix, not just the fast one.
keyPoints: Reduce risk proactively — patch, back up, test restores, review access, and fix visible single points of failure before they fail.
Treat near-misses as free lessons and close the gap before it becomes an incident.
Document systems and write runbooks; undocumented, one-person knowledge is a liability in a rotational service.
Cross-train colleagues and LE Staff so no critical capability lives only in one head; never hoard knowledge for job security.
Adopt a COOP mindset: assume failure and plan how the mission keeps operating, with tested backups and recovery priorities by mission impact.
A backup you have never restored is a hope, not a backup — verify recovery, don't assume it.
Steward government funds, licenses, equipment, and data as if personally accountable; never misuse resources for convenience or unofficial purposes.
Report waste, fraud, and abuse through proper channels (supervisor, management, OIG); don't ignore it, join in, or act as a vigilante.
Run a blameless after-action review after incidents and follow through on systemic fixes — don't skip it because things work again.
Choose the durable fix over the fast one: integrity and accountability over expediency.

flashcards.front: After resolving an outage, the system works again. Skip the review or hold an after-action?
flashcards.back: Hold a blameless after-action review: what happened, why, and what systemic change prevents recurrence — then document and follow through. Skipping it because things work is a weak, expedient response that lets the gap survive.

flashcards.front: You discover a colleague has been falsifying procurement records for IT equipment. What do you do?
flashcards.back: Report it through proper channels — supervisor, management, or the Inspector General (OIG). Integrity requires reporting waste/fraud/abuse. Ignoring it, joining in, or confronting them vigilante-style are all wrong; whistleblower protections exist for this.

flashcards.front: Why is 'a backup you've never restored' considered unreliable?
flashcards.back: Because untested backups routinely fail when needed — corrupt files, missing data, broken procedures. Stewardship means verifying recovery by testing restores, not assuming the backup works. A COOP plan rests on tested fallbacks.

flashcards.front: A teammate keeps critical system knowledge undocumented, in their head. Why is that a problem?
flashcards.back: In a rotational service, undocumented one-person knowledge is a single point of failure and a continuity risk. The steward writes runbooks and cross-trains others, including LE Staff. Hoarding knowledge for job security is self-serving and weak.

flashcards.front: What is a COOP (Continuity of Operations) mindset for an IT officer?
flashcards.back: Assume systems will fail and plan how the mission keeps operating anyway: tested backups, offline fallbacks, communication trees, and recovery priorities tied to mission impact. Ask 'how do we keep operating?' not just 'how do we fix this?'

flashcards.front: You notice a recurring near-miss that hasn't yet caused an outage. Best response?
flashcards.back: Treat the near-miss as a free lesson and close the gap proactively before it becomes an incident. Reactive-only heroics during fires, with no prevention, is the weak pattern; proactive risk reduction is stewardship.

flashcards.front: You have leftover grant funds and could buy extra equipment you don't really need. What's the stewardship choice?
flashcards.back: Don't over-buy. Protect government funds as if personally accountable; spend only on genuine mission need and avoid waste. Convenience- or use-it-or-lose-it-driven spending is a weak response even when permitted.

flashcards.front: In an after-action review, what's wrong with concluding 'the night-shift tech caused it'?
flashcards.back: It's blame, not learning. A blameless review asks why the system allowed the error and what systemic change prevents recurrence. Blaming an individual to close the ticket leaves the real gap open and is a weak response.

flashcards.front: How should recovery be prioritized when multiple systems are down?
flashcards.back: By mission impact, not by rank, ease, or who shouts loudest. Restore the capabilities the mission most depends on first, per your pre-defined recovery priorities — a planning-and-stewardship decision made before the crisis.

flashcards.front: What does 'integrity over expediency' mean in incident response?
flashcards.back: Choosing the durable, correct fix and honest reporting over the fast, convenient, or face-saving option — even when the quick path is tempting and unobserved. Accountability for the real outcome, not just closing the ticket.

flashcards.front: You're rotating out in two months. What's the responsible knowledge-transfer step?
flashcards.back: Document systems and runbooks, update access records, and cross-train your successor and LE Staff so no capability leaves with you. Leaving undocumented systems behind is a stewardship failure in a rotational service.

flashcards.front: Why report waste/fraud/abuse up the chain or to OIG rather than handling it yourself?
flashcards.back: Proper channels ensure a fair, authorized, protected process and proper investigation. Vigilante confrontation can compromise evidence and safety; staying silent makes you complicit. Reporting through channels is the integrity-preserving move.
